1998-10-01.title: Clinton Putting Off Visits to India and Pakistan
1998-10-01.abstract: White House says Pres Clinton has postponed visit to India and Pakistan, whose tests of nuclear devices in May set off alarm around world; says Clinton will not make separate visit to Bangladesh, but will go ahead with plans to attend meeting of Asia Pacific Economic Conference in Malaysia in mid-Nov (M)

1998-10-01.title: U.S. Charges Bin Laden Suspect in Larger Plot
1998-10-01.abstract: Federal prosecutors in Manhattan charge Mamdouh Mahmud Salim with taking part in conspiracy to attack US military sites abroad; Salim is associate of Osama bin Laden, Saudi exile suspected in embassy bombings in Africa; Salim was arrested in Munich, Germany, in Sept; prosecutors say they will seek his extradition (M)

1998-10-01.title: Teheran Journal; Lifting the Veil, Just a Bit, on West's Decadent Art
1998-10-01.abstract: Over 400 important paintings have not been displayed in Iran since 1979 Islamic revolution; paintings, deemed too provocative to be seen, are kept in Museum of Contemporary Art vault; Ali Reza Semiazar, museum's new director, has won permission from Ministry of Culture and Islamic Guidance to put some of Western collection on regular display, reflecting Pres Mohammad Khatami's call for greater cultural openness; photo (M)

1998-10-01.title: On Eve of Key Meeting, I.M.F. Paints a Gloomy Global Picture
1998-10-01.abstract: International Monetary Fund, in unusually pessimistic report, says that it expects Russian economy to contract by 6 percent this year and next, and Japan to sink into its deepest recession since end of World War II; report, issued day before finance ministers from around world arrive in Washington, DC, for annual meetings of World Bank and IMF, also scales back estimates of how fast other economies will grow; it urges 90 percent of world's central banks to cut interest rates in effort to spur growth and calls for greater cuts in US (M)

1998-10-01.title: Hoping to Control Spread of AIDS, China Bans the Sale of Blood
1998-10-01.abstract: New law banning buying and selling of blood takes effect in China on Oct 1, reflecting growing awareness that nation's heavy reliance on paid blood donors is contributing to rapid spread of AIDS (M)

1998-10-02.title: Top U.N. Arms Inspector Tries to Quiet Ex-Iraq Team Member
1998-10-02.abstract: Richard Butler, head of United Nations' Iraqi disarmament commission, says that he has ordered Scott Ritter, American inspector who quit in anger in August, to stop revealing information he gathered on the job (M)

1998-10-02.title: On Election Eve, Doubts Cloud Brazilians' Outlook
1998-10-02.abstract: Brazilians prepare to vote on Oct 4; polls predict that Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso, highly acclaimed for putting inflation under control, will handily win re-election; but behind voters' preferences is a kaleidoscope of doubts and difficulties that Brazilians face as their economy grinds to virtual halt, and they prepare for humiliation of ceding some degree of sovereignty for expected $30 billion in aid from International Monetary Fund; Cardoso's closest rival, Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, union leader from leftist Workers' Party, opposes bailout and contends administration lacks an effective industrial and employment policy; photo (M)

1998-10-02.title: Survivor of Kosovo Massacre Describes the Killing Garden
1998-10-02.abstract: 42-year-old man who says he is lone survivor of a massacre in which 13 ethnic Albanians were summarily shot and killed in Golubovac village in Kosovo recalls how he was herded into a garden with other men and told to lie flat on ground; recounts how police demanded to know who were members of Kosovo Liberation Army, and then started shooting; survivor, father of eight children, is advised by Western diplomats and by researcher for Human Rights Watch not to give his name for fear of jeopardizing his safety; photo; map (M)

1998-10-02.title: Rubin Urges More Disclosure in Global Finance and Making Investors Pay Bailout Share
1998-10-02.abstract: Treasury Secretary Robert E Rubin asserts that United States will press for 'new openess in international finance' that will require private financial institutions to disclose far more information about their loans around world and force investors to pay their share of cost of bailing out troubled nations, speech in NYC to annual conference on the Americas, held by Dow Jones & Co (M)

1998-10-02.title: A FUND OF TROUBLE: A special report.; As Economies Fail, the I.M.F. Is Rife With Recriminations
1998-10-02.abstract: Special Report, A Fund of Trouble, on recriminations and criticisms besetting International Monetary Fund, as world's finance ministers gather in Washington for annual meeting of IMF and World Bank; just about everything that could have gone wrong in world economy has: worst downturn in Japan since World War II, economic meltdown in Russia, depression in Indonesia that is plunging 100 million people below poverty line, and deep fears over what happens next in Latin Amreica; what makes this year's meeting of IMF most remarkable is that harshest criticisms are directed at monetary fund itself, and, by extension, at United States Treasury, which is viewed as power behind the fund; 53 years after IMF was created, it is under attack from all sides, charged not only with worsening a bad situation by misjudging the economics, but with being politically tone-deaf in some of most volatile capitals in world, from Jakarta to Moscow; photos (L)

1998-10-03.title: Tradition or Change?Japan Dithers
1998-10-03.abstract: TOKYO—  Asia's financial crisis may have started in Thailand, but its solution, say many, must begin in Japan.

1998-10-03.title: Cairo Journal; Praising Love and War, Beneath the Pyramids
1998-10-03.abstract: Egypt celebrates 25th anniversary of October 1973 war against Israel, starting festivities with mass wedding of 250 couples in shadow of Great Pyramids; both Egypt and Israel claim victory in the war, which was a turning point in Arab-Israel relations, prompting the first real consideration of concept of land for peace; photo (M)

1998-10-03.title: UBS Chairman Joins Casualty List From Hedge Fund's Setback : 4 Executives At Big Bank Lose Jobs Over Losses
1998-10-03.abstract: FRANKFURT—  The chairman of UBS AG, Europe's biggest bank, and three of his top executives abruptly quit Friday to take responsibility for damaging losses in the near-collapse of a U.S. investment fund.

1998-10-03.title: Republicans Criticize Clinton On Kosovo
1998-10-03.abstract: Several influential Republicans, including Sens Richard G Lugar, Don Nickles and Pete V Domenici, criticize Clinton Administration's approach toward ending crisis in Kosovo, which calls for air strikes in Kosovo and elsewhere in Serbia if Pres Slobodan Milosevic of Yugoslavia does not agree to remove his special police forces from province, permit aid to refugees and begin serious negotiations toward a political settlement (M)

1998-10-03.title: Economy Shift In Russia Worries U.S., Albright Says
1998-10-03.abstract: Secretary of State Madeleine K Albright expresses disappointment at economic confusions within new Russian Government of Prime Minister Yevgeny M Primakov; cites talk about printing new money, indexing wages, imposing price and capital controls and restoring state management of parts of the economy; warns Russia against the 'many voices' there who want to shift emphasis in Russia's interaction with the West from one of partnership to one of assertiveness, opposition and defiance for its own sake (M)

1998-10-04.title: Force in Bosnia Fails at One Task: A Pullout
1998-10-04.abstract: Patrols in Bosnia by American and other NATO forces have become mostly routine, although troops still run into roadblocks, angry crowds and illegal weapons caches, but mission has not figured out how to leave; nobody in Clinton Administration will admit there is no end in sight, for fear of further irritating Congress, but evidence is clear; NATO has broken ground on new headquarters near Sarajevo, UN is spending $34 million to buff up its camps and extend runways at Tuzla; photos; map; many central promises of Dayton accords remain unfulfilled; Sarajevo's streets bustle, but most of country remains economically listless; unified government barely exists, and then only under foreign pressure; NATO commander Gen Wesley K Clark sys 99 percent of military task is completed, but warns of very high risk of fighting if forces dare to go home (M)

1998-10-04.title: Poverty Spreads, and Deepens, in China's Cities
1998-10-04.abstract: Number of city dwellers living in poverty in China, group virtually nonexistent decade ago, is now growing rapidly as unskilled workers lose out in rapid transformation to market economy; millions of urban poor, distinct from poor migrants from countryside, cannoot afford basic food, clothing or shelter; photo of 50-year-old itinerant garbage collector in Xian whose dilapidated, but free, home was demolished to make way for new apartments he cannot afford; photo; old planned economy assigned nearly all urban Chinese to work units, which guaranteed them housing and subsidized food, medical care, pensions and schooling; system has crumbled in last few years as state companies close or downsize or lack funds to pay benefits (M)

1998-10-04.title: In the Caribbean, Support Growing for the Death Penalty
1998-10-04.abstract: Trinidad and Tobago draws international condemnation, but broad support at home, as it withdraws from UN and Inter-American rights conventions over curbs on death penalty; decision to speed up hangings of convicted criminals is widely supported by public alarmed over rising crime rates; map; Trinidad Atty Gen Ramesh L Maharaj says numerous, lengthy appeals drag out process; conventions require commuting to life in prison any death sentence when prisoner has been held more than five years (M)

1998-10-04.title: Feud Eclipses Time of Glory For Leader Of Malaysia
1998-10-04.abstract: Malaysian Prime Min Mahathir Mohamad's record of achievement over last 17 years is threatened by political cat fight stemming from his arrest of heir apparent, Anwar Ibrahim, on charges of corruption and illegal sex acts; Mahathir, 72, fostered economic liberalization, drove Malaysians to be more productive and to embrace high technology, created opportunities for ethnic minorities and sense of national pride; dizzying turnaround has left country teetering economically and politically (M)

1998-10-04.title: New Evidence Ties Sudanese To Bin Laden, U.S. Asserts
1998-10-04.abstract: American intelligence officials report new evidence linking owner of Sudanese factory destroyed by US missile strike to terrorist group backed by Osama bin Laden, suspected mastermind of US embassy bombings in Africa; Salih Idris reportedly has had dealings with Islamic Jihad, which gets money from bin Laden; Idris denies charge (M)

1998-10-05.title: Protests Set for Moscow, but Over What?
1998-10-05.abstract: Communist Party, which currently controls Russian Government and has undergone resurgence by playing on economic discontent, is working hard to cast itself as outsider in Government it helps to run in demonstration planned for Moscow on October 7, and to keep focus of protests on Pres Boris Yeltsin and his policies; photos (M)

1998-10-05.title: JAPANESE TELL U.S. THAT THEIR BANKS ARE IN BIG TROUBLE
1998-10-05.abstract: Japan's top financial officials tell American counterparts country's banking system is acutely short of capital, with top 19 banks in deeper trouble than Tokyo has ever before admitted; Bank of Japan Governor Masaru Hayami tells Treas Sec Robert E Rubin and Federal Reserve chairman Alan Greenspan in Washington meeting that capital supporting banks has dwindled to dangerously low levels, such that they may be banned from operating internationally; other senior Japanese officials, in reflection of confusion surrounding Japan's financial crisis, dispute dispute Hayami's presentation; insist reserve levels have not declined to dangerous levels; conflicting explanations come as financial leaders of world's major economies hold annual meetings of International Monetary Fund and World Bank to grapple with economic crisis many fear has spun out of control; Rubin presses for changes at fund and bank that would improve their ability to head off crises; other officials, including World Bank president James D Wolfensohn, argue that countries should focus their energies on immediate crisis and postpone broader discussion of remaking global financial system (M)

1998-10-05.title: Joseph C. McCarthy, Pilot in '43 Dam Buster Raid, Dies at 79
1998-10-05.abstract: Joseph C McCarthy dies at age of 79; he was lone American in secret unit of Allied pilots that carried out brazen British bombing raid on strategic German dams in 1943; photo (M)

1998-10-05.title: Cardoso Seems to Be Re-elected in Brazil, Averting Runoff
1998-10-05.abstract: Brazilian voters give Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso comfortable lead in first round of balloting, apparently maintaining leadership of country struggling against economic crisis; with 27 percent of vote counted, he appears to have won 51.3 percent of vote, compared with 33.9 percent for main rival, Luiz Inacio lula da Silva; photo (M)

1998-10-05.title: World Bank Worried by Pressure for Quick-Fix Fiscal Action
1998-10-05.abstract: World Bank, amid spread of global economic crisis, fears being transformed from slow-moving development agency into financial fire truck, programmed to squirt streams of cash at countries under speculative attack; finance ministers from world's seven largest economies, attending bank's and IMF's annual meeting in Washington, warn that without radical strengthening of international financial system crisis will spread far beyond Asia and Russia to affect entire developing world; IMF has lowered economic growth forecasts for developing world as whole, predicting that economies will expand by 2.3 percent in 1998 and 3.6 percent in 1999--compared with 5.8 percent in 1997 (M)

1998-10-06.title: Moscow Journal; Ruble Is on 'Tilt,' but Russians Keep Their Balance
1998-10-06.abstract: Moscow Journal describes mix of fatalism and gallows humor with which Russians are facing economic crisis; drawing; photo (M)

1998-10-06.title: Driverless Trains Have Paris Debut
1998-10-06.abstract: Article on new Meteor line of Paris Metro--first to be built since 1935 and first to be fully automated (M)

1998-10-06.title: Serbs Continue Kosovo Terror, Annan Asserts
1998-10-06.abstract: Report by UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan accuses Serbian security forces of continuing punitive operations against ethnic Albanians in Kosovo Province as part of campaign of terror and violence; is unequivocal in conclusions--based on accounts from UN High Commissioner for Refugees, diplomats in region and Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe--bolstering assessments by NATO and American officials; catalogues hallmarks of Serb drive against ethnic Albanians; calls it collective punishment to teach that price of supporting separatists is high and will be even higher in future; says Serbian security forces have used terrror and violence against civilians to force people to flee homes or where they sought refuge, under guise of separating them from fighters of Kosovo Albanian paramilitary units; expresses concern about appearance of Serbian-laid land mines in new areas, away from Serbia's borders with Albania and Macedonia; puts number of displaced people at about 280,000; US envoy Richard C Holbrooke meets with Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic in Belgrade and reportedly warns that he must take further steps to withdraw military units from Kosovo or face NATO attack; US and NATO officials say Yugoslav Army still has substantial forces in Kosovo, despite some withdrawals; NATO Sec Gen Javier Solana says Milosevic has failed to respond adequately to UN Security Council demand for withdrawal of forces and that alliance is ready to act (M)

1998-10-06.title: Latin Americans Say Russian Bailout Leaves Too Little for Them
1998-10-06.abstract: Latin American officials, increasingly angry at what they consider lenient treatment Russia has received in its $4.5 billion bailout by International Monetary Fund, warn programs being offered to protect their own countries are too paltry; criticisms by Mexico's central bank governor, Guillermo Ortiz, noted (M)

1998-10-06.title: Holbrooke Meets Milosevic on Pullback
1998-10-06.abstract: US envoy Richard C Holbrooke meets with Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic in Belgrade; reportedly warns he faces NATO attack unless he withdraws military from Kosovo Province; reportedly told Milosevic he had to insure that 250,000 ethnic Albanian refugees who have fled their homes in fighting in past six months be allowed to return safely; photo (M)

1998-10-07.title: France Detects Iraqi Nerve Gas, Experts Assert
1998-10-07.abstract: Weapons experts say French tests appear to have found traces of chemicals linked to nerve gas VX on Iraqi warheads, finding that would confirm earlier American test results; diplomats charge that France is delaying release of findings to avoid undermining Iraq's push at United Nations to lift economic sanctions; France denies withholding results; says they are expected to be 'consistent' with earlier tests that found no traces of VX (M)

1998-10-07.title: Dissension Erupts at Talks On World Financial Crisis
1998-10-07.abstract: World Bank president, James D Wolfensohn, breaks with United States-International Monetary Fund approach to managing global financial crisis, speech to World Bank-IMF meeting, Washington; holds too little attention is being paid to growing ranks of unemployed and threats to political stability in rush to stabilize currencies and reform economies; speech brings into open months of tensions between sister institutions over strategy for dealing with crisis; Treasury Sec Robert Rubin urges institutions to settle differences; Pres Clinton urges search for way to temper volatile swings of international marketplace; again proposes that major nations set up 'precautionary lines of credit' to help countries caught up in financial turmoil despite adopting tough reforms; Adminstration holds preliminary talks on British Prime Min Tony Blair's call for emergency economic summit meeting; IMF managing direct Michel Camdessus says crisis can be overcome; insists burden of economic reform falls on individual nations; says IMF cannot resume funding Russia until its new Government embraces reform; photo of Pres Clinton (M)

1998-10-07.title: Holbrooke Is Unable to Wrest Concessions From Milosevic
1998-10-07.abstract: United States envoy Richard C Holbrooke fails to extract any concessions on troop pullback from Kosovo Province or return of refugees in talks with Yugoslav President, Slobodan Milosevic; diplomats see plans for more talks as bid by Milosevic to stall NATO air strikes; he has begun using threatened attacks to consolidate support among Serbs; photo (M)

1998-10-07.title: Russia Vows to Block the U.N. From Backing Attack on Serbs
1998-10-07.abstract: Russia vows to veto any UN Security Council authorization of air strikes against Yugoslavia and warns of 'serious international consequences' if NATO uses force to halt ethnic conflict in Kosovo Province; stepped-up involvement in Kosovo crisis comes as Russia is pressing West to rescue its foundering economy; comes amid increasingly open hostility to West, which many Russians blame for promoting policies that have led to economy's near-collapse (M)

1998-10-07.title: Europe Sharpening Its Challenge To the U.S. Over Economic Crisis
1998-10-07.abstract: European governments are challenging United States leadership in addressing world financial crisis as turmoil persists and Congress continues to block new funding for International Monetary Fund; are flexing their muscles as economic power at IMF-World Bank meeting of finance ministers and central bankers, Washington, after year spent largely on sidelines while US worked closely with IMF to curb turmoil; favor shifting more power into existing IMF policy-setting committee, move that would limit direct US influence over fund; score opposition in Republican-controlled Congress to providing full $18 billion sought by Pres Clinton for IMF; are more sympathetic than US to World Bank stress on poverty-fighting over austerity programs and to its questioning of blanket opposition to controls on international flow of capital; photo (M)

1998-10-08.title: Wrangling Over I.M.F. Bill at Fever Pitch
1998-10-08.abstract: Clinton Administration and Congressional Republicans are working frantically on bill to give International Monetary Fund added $18 billion as fund seeks to avert global financial meltdown; agree money is urgently needed; debate curbs sought by conservatives on future lending by IMF, which has been criticized by both Republicans and Democrats for mismanaging global financial crisis (M)

1998-10-08.title: Meeting of World Finance Leaders Ends, With No Grand Strategy but Many Ideas
1998-10-08.abstract: World's finance ministers and central bankers end gloomy International Monetary Fund-World Bank joint meeting, Washington, with no strategy to lift global markets out of downward spiral, but plethora of new ideas; participants repeatedly blamed Japan for problems, as well as hedge funds and currency speculators (M)

1998-10-08.title: Korean Leader, Visiting Japan, Urges Healing of Old Wounds
1998-10-08.abstract: South Korean Pres Kim Dae Jung arrives in Japan for state visit; calls for closer partnership and end to longstanding tensions between neighbors; Japanese officials seem ready to reciprocate by making unusually humble apology for colonial past (M)

1998-10-08.title: Roger Vivier Is Dead at 90; Raised Heels to New Heights
1998-10-08.abstract: Roger Vivier, leading shoe designer whose innovations in heel and toe shapes changed silhouette of women's shoes, dies at age 90; photo (M)

1998-10-08.title: Belgrade Bars U.N. War-Crimes Investigators
1998-10-08.abstract: United Nations war crimes tribunal, Hague, says Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic's Government bars United Nations from probing reported war crimes in Kosovo, Serbia, and denies visas sought by tribunal; says Government has threatened to close tribunal's office in Belgrade if court continues to collect evidence on alleged Kosovo massacres (S)

1998-10-09.title: Republicans List Terms for I.M.F. Payout
1998-10-09.abstract: Republican Congressional leaders present Clinton Adm with list of lending conditions that they want imposed on IMF in order for Congress to approve $18 billion in new money for IMF; list is not made public, but Congressional officials say it includes demand that IMF open its books for public scrutiny, that it end its practices of lending to foreign govts at below-market interest rates, and that it shorten lending perod on most loans (M)

1998-10-09.title: U.S. May Ask Death Penalty in Embassy Bombings
1998-10-09.abstract: Federal prosecutors in Manhattan say that Govt is considering asking for death penalty for two of suspects arrested after bombings of United States Embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in August; suspects for whom death penalty may be sought are Mohamed Rashed Daoud al'Owhali and Mohammed Saddiq Odeh (M)

1998-10-09.title: A Champagne Evening Awash in Gloom
1998-10-09.abstract: Meeting of international finance ministers and central bankers in Washington ends in gloom, with world financial crisis hanging over IMF meeting (M)

1998-10-09.title: Iran's Forces Said to Clash With Afghans Along Border
1998-10-09.abstract: Iran says that its troops clashed with Taliban forces along Afghan border and inflicted heavy casualties there in skirmish between neighbors who have been in a tense standoff; reported fighting, which follows more than two months of bellicose statements from both sides, would be first between Iran and Taliban, whose rival interpretations of Islam have contributed to open hostility; map (M)

1998-10-09.title: New Kosovo Refugees, Many Ailing, Scramble to Survive
1998-10-09.abstract: Kosovo refugees, many ailing, scramble to survive in hills, woods and fields; Doctors Without Borders, relief agency, says newest refugees of Kosovan crisis are the worst off; warns in report to diplomatic monitors that refugees have perilously little food and that carcasses of animals shot by troops threaten creek water that is used for drinking, cooking and washing; unlike longer-term refugees new contingent has had no time to put up reinforced tents with wood floors; they sleep sandwiched 8 to 10 in open-air trailers covered with flimsy swaths of plastic; photo (M)

1998-10-10.title: A U.S. Message to Milosevic Turns Into Full-Dress Talks
1998-10-10.abstract: American envoy Richard C Holbrooke, who came to Belgrade, Serbia, to warn Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic to comply on Kosovo or face NATO attack, is in midst of full-dress talks; reportedly makes some progress; photo (M)p

1998-10-10.title: Schroder Tells Clinton He Backs Action by NATO in Kosovo
1998-10-10.abstract: German Chancellor-elect Gerhard Schroder, meeting with Pres Clinton, Washington, says he would back decision by caretaker German Government to authorize NATO use of force over Kosovo, if necessary; indicates any authorization for use of German troops and planes against Serbia should await seating of newly elected Parliament; Clinton stresses importance of forthcoming NATO vote to authorize use of force (M)

1998-10-10.title: Chang Chong-Jen, 93, Model for Character in Tintin Comic
1998-10-10.abstract: Chang Chong-Jen, Chinese painter and sculptor who was model for character in Tintin cartoons, dies at age 93; photo (M)

1998-10-10.title: Italy's Center-Left Government Is Toppled by One Vote
1998-10-10.abstract: Prime Min Romano Prodi's center-left government loses confidence vote, 313-312, suggesting return to chronic political instability in Italy; Prodi resigns but will remain in charge as caretaker until new government is formed; photo (M)

1998-10-10.title: Beijing Journal; A New Epic With a History of Its Own
1998-10-10.abstract: China lets director Chen Kaige hold world film premiere of his historical epic about ancient battles that led to China's unification in Great Hall of the People, Beijing; English title of film, set for United States distribution, has not been chosen; photo (M)

1998-10-11.title: A Jew's Odyssey From Catholic Nun to Saint
1998-10-11.abstract: Pope John Paul II will pronounce Edith Stein, renowned Jewish intellectual who became Carmelite nun and died at Auschwitz, a saint and martyr for Roman Catholic faith at Mass in St Peter's Square; ever since she was beatified in 1987, many Jewish groups and members of her family have questioned Vatican's logic and sensitivity, arguing that Stein was sent to gas chambers because she was a Jew, and not because she was a nun; photo (M)

1998-10-11.title: Some in Japan Fear Authors of Subway Attack Are Regaining Ground
1998-10-11.abstract: Security officials and Japanese and American terrorism experts say Aum Shinrikyo, cult that planted nerve gas in Tokyo subway three years ago, killing 12 people and injuring thousands more, is back; they say cult is regrouping, recruiting new members at home and abroad and raising vast sums of money; State Department has designated it terrorist group, although it has not been linked to any illegal acts since 1995; resurgence of sect is due partly to Japan's unwillingness to ban it; photos (M)

1998-10-11.title: NATO Nears Final Order To Approve Kosovo Strike
1998-10-11.abstract: Western officials say NATO is ready to approve 'activation order' on Oct 12, opening way for its military commanders to order air strikes against Serbian targets in Kosovo and perhaps in Serbian heartland (M)

1998-10-11.title: Aid for Sudan's Hungry Keeps War Well Fed
1998-10-11.abstract: World's governments, faced with prospect of pictures of emaciated, malnourished children as wellas thousands of deaths in southern Sudan, launches Operation Lifeline Sudan and appeals for funds to meet the 'emergency' in 1989; tons of food was delivered to suffering, where civil war was root cause of starvation; nearly a decade and more than $2 billion later, situation is virtually identical, despite what Operation Lifeline describes as one of largest relief operations ever; officials in Washington and at relief organizations wonder whether aid is helping perpetuate fighting between African Christians and Muslim Arabs, giving factions what they need to carry on war in which both sides are guilty of atrocities; photo (M)

1998-10-11.title: A Coca-Trade Jungle Town Trapped by Colombia's Strife
1998-10-11.abstract: Bloody struggle over future of Colombia, raging between shadowy groups of men and women with masks and false names, is closing in on Calamar, main transit point in coca trade; many residents have not left the town in a year; only road out is controlled by paramilitaries, who have established checkpoint a few miles from nearest military base and charge coca growers and food suppliers simply to get their products in and out of town; residents fear dying for simply coming from Calamar, which paramilitaries label a rebel support base; map; photo (M)

1998-10-12.title: Putting Aside Activism, Students In Korea Focus on Hunt for Jobs : Campus Life:Work, No Fray
1998-10-12.abstract: SEOUL—  On the third floor of the student center at Korea University, Lee Han Byul, deputy chief of a student union affiliated with a national student organization noted for sometimes radical activism, talks earnestly about his country's problems.

1998-10-12.title: Deep-Sea Clues to an Ancient Culture Discovered
1998-10-12.abstract: Deep-sea submersible, operated by Odyssey Marine Explorations Inc of Tampa, Fla, discovers hundreds of amphoras, clay storage jars of antiquity, deep in western Mediterranean; find has been tentatively identified as typical of Phoenician colony at Carthage (near modern-day Tunis) around fifth century BC near peak of Phoenician influence; discovery is stirring great scholarly interest because so little is known of Phoenicians, who were successful traders during first millenium BC who sailed mainly out of area that is now Lebanon, spreading colonies along Mediterranean coast; despite their early success, subsequent waves of civilization wiped out most traces of them; map; photo (M)

1998-10-12.title: U.S. Policies: Testing Time
1998-10-12.abstract: News analysis of effects of White House sex scandal on US domestic and foreign policy; with Pres Clinton distracted and his moral authority questioned, there is much room for US allies, Congress and even some major corporations to ignore, deflect or defy him; defiance by Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic over Serbia, refusal of Republican-controlled Congress to fulfill US obligations to International Monetary Fund and UN, and Clinton's failure to persuade major oil companies to build oil pipeline from Caspian Sea through Turkey, declared American strategic priority, discussed (M)

1998-10-12.title: Pope Canonizes Jew Who Became Nun and Died at Auschwitz
1998-10-12.abstract: Pope John Paul II canonizes Edith Stein, Jewish intellectual who became Carmelite nun and died at Auschwitz; recalls her Jewish roots and suffering of Jews in Mass at St Peter's in Rome (M)

1998-10-12.title: India's Hottest Political Issue: The Price of Onions
1998-10-12.abstract: Shortage of onions is producing long lines, high prices and anger in many Indian cities, threatening to topple Hindu nationalist state governments in Delhi and Rajasthan in November elections; maps; photo (M)

1998-10-13.title: Russia Sees NATO Raids as Imperiling Cooperation With West
1998-10-13.abstract: Russia mounts last-ditch campaign today to avert NATO air strikes against Yugoslavia, warning that such action could bring end to its cooperation with Western alliance; intensity of its diplomatic effort reflects ascension of new Prime Min Yevgeny M Primakov, former Foreign Min whose efforts have always been to circumscribe NATO's influence and contest American dominance; he has often cast Moscow as wounded great power in open-ended struggle with West (M)

1998-10-13.title: NATO OPENS WAY TO START BOMBING IN SERB PROVINCE
1998-10-13.abstract: US and its allies approve order clearing way for NATO to begin bombing campaign in Serbian province of Kosovo within four days, but White House says agreement to end crisis with Serbs appears imminent; senior American and NATO officials say significant progress was made in talks between special US envoy, Richard C Holbrooke and Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic over oppression of ethnic Albanians; say Milosevic has agreed to end all police and military operations in Kosovo, withdraw forces in verifiable way and make unilateral statement pledging partial autonomy in province; say he appears ready to allow force of 2,000 monitors, mostly civilian, into Kosovo; caution that differences remain over exact makeup of international mission; say Milosevic's reluctance to pledge cooperation with international war crimes investigations is also outstanding issue; Pres Clinton expresses guarded optimism over developments, noting Milosevic has failed to keep promises in past; says while US and allies prefer to settle issue peacefully, military force will be used if Milosevic fails to keep word; photos (M)

1998-10-13.title: A Rough Trip for Yeltsin Adds to Worries About Health
1998-10-13.abstract: Pres Boris Yeltsin abruptly cuts short three-day visit to Central Asia after doctor announced he was suffering from bronchitis and slight fever; Kremlin's record of covering up his health problems leaves commentators questioning whether Yeltsin's illness was more serious; has compounded sense that nation lacks fully functioning leader during one of its gravest economic crises; photo (M)

1998-10-13.title: Evidence to Deny 6 Iraqis Asylum May Be Weak, Files Show
1998-10-13.abstract: Newly declassified Government documents show US has relied on weak and unsubstantiated evidence that has never been fully investigated to detain six Iraqi dissidents who worked with Central Intelligence Agency against Pres Saddam Hussein; Iraqis, removed in 1996 by Clinton Administration along with 600 other CIA-supported dissidents and additional 5,000 Iraqis and Kurds as Hussein's forces attacked opposition groups in northern Iraq, have been in legal limbo in Immigration and Naturalization Service center in Los Angeles; have been denied asylum in secret hearing in which they were not allowed to see evidence, hear testimony or even know charges keeping them in custody; declassified documents show many of charges against them stem from unsubstantiated allegations made to FBI by rival Iraqis combined with other fragmentary evidence like family connections to other suspected foreign agents (M)

1998-10-13.title: U.S. Catholic Seminarians Turning to Orthodoxy
1998-10-13.abstract: Young American men entering priesthood are far more conservative and tradition-bound than many of their elders and most American Roman Catholics, according to bishops, priests and church surveys; in sharp swing of pendulum, seminarians are rejecting liberalism and rebellion that swept Catholic Church after Vatican II and American society in 1960's and 1970's; while shift back to orthodoxy is welcomed by many church leaders after decades of confusion and conflict, others worry that trend could create rift between more rigid, conformist clergy and majority of American Catholics, who routinely ignore Rome's teachings on major issues; surge in enrollments at Pontifical North American College of Rome, seminary for Americans preparing for priesthood, to highest level in quarter-century, discussed; photo (M)

1998-10-14.title: U.S. Balks at Cleanup of Hazardous Canal Zone Sites
1998-10-14.abstract: For nearly a century, the United States military has used the jungle terrain surrounding the Panama Canal as a testing ground for explosives and chemical weapons. Now, a little more than a year before those ranges must be handed back to Panama, Washington is balking at cleaning up some of the most hazardous of the ordnance its troops are leaving behind.    The deadly debris consists mostly of bombs and other unexploded munitions that for decades were tested at three ranges near the west bank of the canal. But the dangerous materials either tested or stored here also include lethal nerve gases, mustard and phosgene gases dating back to World War II, biological agents and, for a short time earlier this decade, depleted uranium projectiles.

1998-10-14.title: Prototype of Peace via Trade in Gaza
1998-10-14.abstract: The grandly named Gaza Industrial Estate, an unprepossessing 120-acre collection of vacant factory sheds and water towers rising from the ragged flatlands east of Gaza City, is an unlikely focus for the highly charged, high-stakes Middle East peace negotiations.    Fenced off from potential markets by the barbed wire of the Israeli border, the park has been ready for business for nearly a year. But security-conscious Israeli officials had been unwilling to guarantee the unimpeded flow of goods that an industrial park needs to attract and sustain investment.

1998-10-14.title: U.N. Tries to Cajole Iraq Into Cooperating on Arms Inspections
1998-10-14.abstract: The Security Council called on Iraq today to resume cooperation with United Nations weapon inspectors, alluding to prospects for faster progress on easing economic sanctions against Baghdad if it heeds the call.    That and several somewhat conciliatory statements by various members of the 15-member Council followed a periodic review today of Iraq's compliance in eliminating its weapons of mass destruction.

1998-10-14.title: London Journal; Has England Lost Its Identity? Or Just Its Cliches?
1998-10-14.abstract: The English, past masters at turning the raised eyebrow and the cold shoulder on others, are suddenly feeling looked down upon and left out in their own land.    In the last year the three other regions that make up Britain -- Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland -- are putting in place new legislatures to deal with parochial affairs no longer considered the preserve of the British Parliament in London. Some in England, traditionally the ruling power in the kingdom, are now thinking that maybe it should have one too.

1998-10-14.title: Republicans Meet Aides To Clinton On I.M.F.
1998-10-14.abstract: The Clinton Administration and Congressional Republican leaders met today to hammer out the final details of compromise legislation that would provide the International Monetary Fund with $18 billion in exchange for new restrictions on its loans to foreign governments.    Congressional officials said that the deal, part of an overall budget agreement between the Administration and Capitol Hill expected to be announced this week, would require the monetary fund to stop making many of its loans at below-market interest rates and compel borrowers to repay the loans within two and a half years.

1998-10-15.title: Set All of England's Mink Free! Oops. That's Killing Them.
1998-10-15.abstract: Over 2,000 mink set free when animal liberationists broke locks on cages of Onneley, Eng, mink farm recently wreaked havoc on surrounding countryside; Agricultural Ministry, while expressing ethical objections to mink industry, deplores attack, noting mink pose threat to other animals and are not prepared to fend for selves (M)

1998-10-15.title: Col. Gaston de Bonneval, 86, De Gaulle's Closest Confidant
1998-10-15.abstract: Col Gaston de Bonneval, French Resistance fighter who spent two decades as Charles de Gaulle's confidant, dies at age 86; photo (M)

1998-10-15.title: In Protest, Clinton Downgrades Visit to Malaysia : U.S. 'Horrified' About Anwar
1998-10-15.abstract: SINGAPORE—  The United States intensified its criticism of Malaysia on Wednesday over the treatment of the dismissed deputy prime minister, Anwar Ibrahim.

1998-10-15.title: 2,000 Monitors to Go to Kosovo, but Their Power Is Unclear
1998-10-15.abstract: Much remains unresolved about exact role and powers of Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe, which will send 2,000 unarmed civilians to Kosovo, Serbia, to verify that Yugoslav military and police forces end crackdown and let ethnic Albanians return to homes under accord worked out by United States envoy Richard C Holbrooke and Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; organization chairman Bronislaw Geremek, Poland's Foreign Minister, is to meet with Serbian officials to clear way for monitors to arrive by end of week; American William Walker is leading candidate to head mission, which will be largely European (M)

1998-10-15.title: Bombay Journal; India Relishes the Novels She Sprinkles With Spice
1998-10-15.abstract: Shobha De, India's biggest-selling novelist writing in English, has built devoted following of middle-class readers; none of her racy, commercially successful novels has been published in United States; De, who has just come out with her memoirs, comments, interview; photo (M)

1998-10-16.title: Deal to Pay Back American U.N. Dues Collapses Over Abortion
1998-10-16.abstract: Clinton Administration and Congressional leaders say deal for US to pay $200 million in back dues to UN as part of overall Federal budget package collapsed after issue became entangled again in abortion politics; Republicans made funds contingent on White House agreement to bar Federal grants to family-planning organizations that lobby for abortion rights overseas; Speaker Gingrich says UN money will be included in State Department authorization; urges Clinton to sign despite 'tiny' abortion provision (S)

1998-10-16.title: Clinton Officials Say I.M.F. Deal Expands Strategies to Halt Crisis
1998-10-16.abstract: Clinton Administration says budget agreement with Republican Congress that will inject $18 billion into IMF's depleted coffers will also give officials political leeway to try new strategies in global financial turmoil; accord paves way for other nations to increase their contributions, which will enable IMF to help Brazil with at least $30 billion; Pres Clinton's proposal for multibillion-dollar credit lines for countries is also boosted; Republicans have seen IMF as incompetent, but Repr Dick Armey, in talks with Sec Robert E Rubin, has been more conciliatory; highlights on review of IMF and its policies (M)

1998-10-16.title: YUGOSLAV LEADER IS NOT COMPLYING WITH KOSOVO PACT
1998-10-16.abstract: Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic has not complied with NATO demands to withdraw troops and police forces from Kosovo; heavily armed Serb police continue to saunter around camp in sight of ethnic Albanian villages as NATO reiterates threat of air strikes; map; Sec Gen Javier Solana, joined by NATO commander Gen Wesley K Clark, arrives in Belgrade and complains that Milosevic is not complying with agreement brokered by Richard Holbrooke; White House spokesman concurs; relief workers and diplomatic monitors are at same time returning to Kosovo, suggesting NATO will not carry out attacks; monitors confirm that Serb military presence remains intense (M)

1998-10-16.title: Australian Patient's Transplanted Hand Is Doing Well in France
1998-10-16.abstract: Clint Hallam of Australia will leave French hospital with his transplanted hand; condition is reported good, although longtime outcome will not be known for year (S)

1998-10-16.title: A Soft, Voluminous Look Is Edging Out Skinny Silhouette : Loosening Up on Slim Chic
1998-10-16.abstract: PARIS—  Look down any chilly street this fall in Paris and what will you see: beautiful young girls with Mona Lisa smiles in crunchy knit tunics and A-line floor-sweeping skirts preferably in boiled wool. The fashion faithful these days is sliding into a new silhouette, one that makes top to bottom skinny chic and stilettos look like old hash.

1998-10-17.title: REPRIEVE BY NATO ALLOWS MILOSEVIC ANOTHER 10 DAYS
1998-10-17.abstract: NATO grants Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic a reprieve, extending by 10 days its deadline for him to withdraw military and police forces cracking down on ethnic Albanians in province of Kosovo; in face of abundant evidence that Milosevic has not yet kept his pledges to withdraw forces, NATO's reprs in Brussels give him until Oct 27 to comply or face threat of NATO military attacks; they say reprieve brings time for international observers to arrive on ground in Kosovo; they also say it will allow NATO to start surveillance flights; photo (M)

1998-10-17.title: Catholic Scholars Call Message a Timely Critique
1998-10-17.abstract: Analysis of Pope John Paul II's latest encyclical, which amplifies historic church position that faith and reason cannot be separated; some scholars comment (M)

1998-10-17.title: New U.S. Disclosures on Tanzania Bombing
1998-10-17.abstract: FBI, in rare public statement about August bombings of two American embassies in Africa, says that two vehicles had been used in attack on Embassy in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania; also indicates that it it has identified new suspects in blast in Dar es Salaam, and that they are at large (S)

1998-10-17.title: Nostalgic, John Paul II Celebrates 20-Year Reign
1998-10-17.abstract: John Paul II celebrates his 20th anniversary as Pope; appears at window at St Peter's Basilica as tens of thousands of well-wishers gather beneath to hear and watch by live television hookup the ringing of bells of Crakow Cathedral, just as they had rung at same time on Oct 16, 1978; photo of Pope mingling with crowd of 25,000 Polish pilgrims gathered in St Peter's Square (M)

1998-10-17.title: Leftist Leader to Try to Assemble a New Government in Italy
1998-10-17.abstract: Massimo D'Alema, former Communist and leader of largest leftist party in Italy, is selected to try to form a new Govt; D'Alema, is chosen after Acting Prime Min Romano Prodi, who resigned after losing confidence vote last week, failed to gather enough support in Parliament to form a new Govt (M)

1998-10-18.title: Political Feuds Rack Haiti: So Much for Its High Hopes
1998-10-18.abstract: Long political squabble between Jean-Bertrand Aristide and other leaders of fracturing Lavalas coalition has left Haiti without working government, stalled foreign aid programs and disillusioned and exasperated foreign allies; repeated United States efforts to end impasse have failed; civil servants go unpaid in cities, while only private relief organizations attend to rural social problems; Pres Rene Preval, Aristide ally, lacks votes in Parliament to name Prime Minister to succeed Rosny Smarth, who resigned in June 1997; former allies charge Aristide tries to install dictatorship; photo (M)

1998-10-18.title: Higher Hopes In Baghdad For Ending U.N. Embargo
1998-10-18.abstract: Iraqi officials seem confident that they are closer than ever to easing of United Nations sanctions and that they can avert military strike even if they block arms inspections; most foreign diplomats agree Pres Saddam Hussein has come long way in shifting burden of making case for sanctions to United States and Unscom, UN body charged with dismantling Iraq's weapons of mass destruction (M)

1998-10-18.title: Clinton Returns to Mideast Summit in Maryland
1998-10-18.abstract: Pres Clinton, trying to force pace of Mideast accord, meets separately with Palestinian and Israeli delegations, Wye Mills, Md (M)

1998-10-18.title: Rumors and Secrecy Cloud Issue: Is Qaddafi O.K.?
1998-10-18.abstract: Recent barrage of unusually bizarre rumors about assassination attempts on Libyan leader Muammar el-Qaddafi, who is now hobbling on crutches, has people trying hard to piece together facts amid customary cloud of secrecy; clues include postponed trip, broken leg, reports of roadside ambush and untimely death of provincial governor; photo (M)

1998-10-18.title: Arab-Israel Talks: Wary Dance on a Narrow Ledge
1998-10-18.abstract: Analysis of peace talks in United States between Israelis and Palestinians; bitter 19-month freeze in peace process has left Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu wary of obligation to cede land to Palestinians and Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat wary of cracking down on terror (M)

1998-10-19.title: Officials Accused of Atrocities Losing Places to Hide, Scholars Say
1998-10-19.abstract: Legal scholars say arrest of former Chilean dictator Gen Augusto Pinochet by Scotland Yard on warrant by Spanish magistrate shows growing significance of international human rights law; say it suggests that officials accused of atrocities have fewer places to hide, even if they are carrying diplomatic passports (M)

1998-10-19.title: Arrest Raises New Issues On Tracking Rights Crimes
1998-10-19.abstract: Former Chilean strongman, Gen Augusto Pinochet, remains sequestered under police guard in London following arrest for war crimes on warrant by Spanish magistrate; is awaiting potentially devastating court hearing to weigh extradition to Spain to stand trial for human rights violations committed during 17-year dictatorship, from 1973 to 1990; detention leaves Labor Govt of Prime Min Tony Blair embarrassed by diplomatic implications of arrest that has enraged Government of Chile, which Britain regards as ally, and presents European officials with uncomfortable implications for handling of human rights violators closer to home (M)

1998-10-19.title: MIDEAST  MEETING WILL BE CONTINUED INTO ANOTHER DAY
1998-10-19.abstract: Peace talks between Israeli Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu and Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat continue past original October 18 deadline at Wye Conference Center in Maryland, despite more than 20 hours of personal engagement by Pres Clinton; overall mood is called serious; US officials remain optimistic; Israeli official reports progress on different issues, including security, but no complete agreement on any major issues, specifically Israel's withdrawal from more of West bank and commitment from Palestinians to change their charter, which calls for destruction of Israel; photo (M)

1998-10-19.title: Serb Forces Leaving Kosovo, But at a Slow Pace, U.S. Says
1998-10-19.abstract: Yugoslavia begins withdrawing forces from Kosovo Province in Serbia in what Clinton Administration and NATO officials call significant step by Pres Slobodan Milosevic toward complying with demands to end military campaign against ethnic Albanians; officials express concern over slow pace with which Milosevic Govt is abiding by recently-brokered agreement, reached under threat of NATO air strikes against Yugoslav military and police forces (M)

1998-10-19.title: India and Pakistan Agree Only to Future Talks
1998-10-19.abstract: Indian and Pakistani diplomats end three days of talks in Islamabad, agreeing only to keep talking to each other about ways to reduce risk of nuclear conflict between them; present proposals to dispel mistrust and enmity that have characterized relationship since Pakistan gained independence 51 years ago (M)

1998-10-20.title: Pakistani Premier Prevails in Clash With General
1998-10-20.abstract: Gen Jehangir Karamat, Chief of Staff of Pakistan's Army, is uncharacteristically forced to resign after publicly criticizing Prime Min Nawaz Sharif and proposing stronger military role in making policy; Government has been able to exercise authority over military without dire consequences because of profound changes in political structure since landslide election of Sharif 18 months ago that have strengthened prime minister while weakening army; generals have ruled Pakistan for almost half of 51-year history, and prime ministers have been dismissed in earlier clashes; photo (M)

1998-10-20.title: Sharon's Muscle Evokes Hopes and Some Fears
1998-10-20.abstract: Israeli Foreign Min Ariel Sharon, veteran politician and soldier of Arab-Israeli wars, is widely regarded as key player who could make or break Maryland peace summit between Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu and Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, and feelings toward him have been distinctly ambivalent; hopes that his recent appointment as Foreign Minister could make him tough pragmatist in working through functioning deal with Palestinians have been tempered by awareness that he is also hard nationalist who was at forefront of creating West Bank settlements of 1970's and 1980's, voted against Government's decision to withdraw from most of Hebron, has opposed any withdrawal from more than 9 percent of West Bank territory and still regards Yasir Arafat as terrorist whose hand he will not shake; photo (M)

1998-10-20.title: In Boost for Left Wing, Stollmann Won't Take Economics Portfolio : Free-Marketeer Quits Schroeder's Team
1998-10-20.abstract: FRANKFURT—  Germany's new government lost its most outspoken advocate of economic change when Jost Stollmann abruptly withdrew as the next economics minister on Monday.

1998-10-20.title: Prodi Takes a Swipe At D'Alema for Way Majority Was Formed : Italy Gives Mandate To Former Communist
1998-10-20.abstract: ROME—  Massimo D'Alema became the first former Communist to lead a Western European government on Monday when he succeeded in obtaining the support he needed to form Italy's 56th postwar government.

1998-10-20.title: Ex-Communist at Italy's Helm May, or May Not, Vex the Church
1998-10-20.abstract: Roman Catholic Church may play important role in any political compromise that may result in accession of Massimo D'Alma, former Communist who heads largest leftist party in Italy, to post of Prime Minister; remains powerful political constituency; campaigned vehemently against Communist candidates during cold war and hardly relishes seeing former Communist in power (M)

1998-10-21.title: New Delhi Journal; Sacred Cows Are Wily Too; Just Try Catching One
1998-10-21.abstract: Some 40,000 cows, animal sacred to Hindus, roam streets of New Delhi, often blocking traffic and scavenging through garbage; are threatened by plastic garbage bags, which clog their digestive tracts; cow catchers, who take cows to refuge, often face resistance both from animals and onlookers; photo (M)

1998-10-21.title: Beersheba Suspect Confesses to Other Terrorist Attacks, Israeli Army Aides Say
1998-10-21.abstract: Israeli Army says Salem Rajab al-Sarsour, Hamas militant arrested in grenade attack on Beersheba bus terminal, has confessed to September grenade attack on army post in Hebron and fatal August stabbing of elderly rabbi in Hebron; says he used grenades intended for Jerusalem attack when he became frustrated at failure to get work in Beersheba; Hamas's armed wing claims credit for attack; Palestinian security aide say he acted as individual and attack should not reflect negatively on Palestinians' commitment to cracking down on terrorists (M)

1998-10-21.title: Pact Reached on Shape of German Coalition
1998-10-21.abstract: German Chancellor-elect Gerhard Schroder reaches accord with Green Party on shape of new left-of-center coalition Government; six leading economic institutes say his economic program will do little to solve nation's severe unemployment problems; new government will be younger, more female and in many ways more diverse than under Chancellor Helmut Kohl; Green Party will hold key posts for first time, with its leader, Joscka Fischer, named Foreign Minister and 'vice chancellor' (M)

1998-10-21.title: Siberia as Winter Comes: Hungry, Cold and Broke
1998-10-21.abstract: Concern is growing in Siberia that costly effort to keep Russia's Arctic region's eight million people fed and warm is cracking under economic strain; Russian Government is too broke to meet full cost of sending supplies; regional governments are picking up some of slack, even as ruble's crash has sent prices skyrocketing; many regions are also broke and some areas, especially along Russia's long border with Alaska, face winter with few or no supplies; there is talk of evacuating villages; maps; graphs of food output; photos (M)

1998-10-21.title: Saying Salinas Aided Traffickers, Swiss Seize $90 Million
1998-10-21.abstract: Swiss officials seize over $90 million from Raul Salinas de Gortari, brother of former Pres Carlos Salinas de Gortari of Mexico, after police probe finds he filled Swiss bank accounts with money he got for protecting drug shipments into US; ask Britain to seize added $23.4 million deposited there; find almost 80 witnesses who say they saw Salinas meet with traffickers, introduce them to politicians and get suitcases full of cash; Salinas, jailed in Mexico, denies charges; Swiss file no criminal charges and decline to charge his wife, Paulina Castanon (M)

1998-10-22.title: Taliban Aide Casts Doubt on Giving Up bin Laden
1998-10-22.abstract: Amb Abdelhakim Mujahid, senior Taliban official, says Afghanistan will not extradite Osama bin Laden, accused by United States of planning bombings of American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania, interview at United Nations; says Muslim religious authorities will weigh response should US provide credible proof of his involvement; says bin Laden is barred from activities against other nations (M)

1998-10-22.title: Ariane-5 Success Follows Pair of Failures : Rocket Launch Keeps Europe Alive in Space
1998-10-22.abstract: PARIS—  Ariane-5, the new European launch rocket, went into space Wednesday, putting into orbit a symbolic payload, a dummy telecommunications satellite, and, more important, Europe's hopes of keeping its leadership in the increasingly competitive global market for commercial space launchings.

1998-10-22.title: Fireworks At Wye As Dance Goes On
1998-10-22.abstract: Tension, frustration and brinksmanship apparently have been building at Mideast peace talks at sylvan and secluded Wye Plantation, Maryland, erupting in Israeli threats to leave; feints, image and politics seem to be at least as important as substance as Israeli Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu comes under intensive pressure; he must either accept United States peace plan and face rebellion from his constituents, reject plan and be denounced by political opponents and much of world or play for more time, option both Pres Clinton and Palestinians seek to cut off; photos (M)

1998-10-22.title: Big Cash Infusion Aims to Rebuild Anemic C.I.A.
1998-10-22.abstract: Congress and Pres Clinton, heeding warning from Central Intelligence Agency Dir George J Tenet that CIA needs big infusion of money and talent to remain relevant, approves $1.8 billion supplemental appropriation in addition to secret 'emergency assistance' approved in Apr; Rep Porter J Goss, former CIA operations officer, sees long-term rebuilding effort, interview; photo (M)

1998-10-22.title: Stalled Accord: Main Points in Dispute
1998-10-22.abstract: Key points in talks on new Israeli-Palestinian accord focus on Israeli withdrawal from more West Bank land, security issues, custody of Palestinians accused of attacking Israelis, revision of Palestian charter and steps to curb anti-Israeli incitement (M)

1998-10-23.title: World Briefing
1998-10-23.abstract: EUROPE    ITALY: BUDGET INTACT -- Italy's new Prime Minister, Massimo D'Alema, left, presented to Parliament a stay-the-course program intended to reassure Italians concerned by the fact that he is the first former Communist to head a government. Mr. D'Alema, who had already promised to adhere to the same 1999 budget drafted by his center-left predecessor, Romano Prodi, also indicated that he would not give in to Communists' demands for a 35-hour work week.   Alessandra Stanley (NYT)

1998-10-23.title: From Palestinian or Israeli, The Attitude Is Skepticism
1998-10-23.abstract: Palestinians and Israelis, be they religious or secular, are seen in complete accord on one item: Wye peace conference is not Oslo, in their eyes; they indicate that any legal agreement that would be signed at Wye Plantation in Maryland by two leaders who deeply mistrust each other would not be cause for either panic or celebration engendered by Oslo peace accord in 1993; hoto (M)cve

1998-10-23.title: Guatemala Cover-Up Charged in Killing of Bishop
1998-10-23.abstract: Political controversy in Guatemala surrounding murder nearly six months ago of Juan Gerardi, Roman Catholic bishop and outspoken human rights advocate, continues to grow, while solution to his slaying appears further away than ever; despite mounting evidence to the contrary, Guatemalan Govt continues to maintain that killing was a crime without a political motive; however, Roman Catholic Church and human rights groups contend that elements of Guatemalan armed forces were involved in killing, and point to increasingly bizarre turns case has taken as proof of official cover-up; as result, Gerardi's death has become a major test of 1996 peace accord that brought an end to 36 years of civil war in Guatemala and was supposed to install principle of civilian supremacy; photos of Gerardi and of Rev Mario Orantes Najera, who has been charged with killing (L)

1998-10-23.title: Two Foreign Affairs Agencies Are Eliminated
1998-10-23.abstract: Arms Control and Disarmament Agency and United States Information Agency are abolished under catchall spending bill signed by Pres Clinton; their responsibilties will pass to State Dept (S)

1998-10-23.title: Office-Block Brussels / Bureaucracy Is Their Business : Eurocapital in Search of a Human Dimension
1998-10-23.abstract: BRUSSELS—  The European Union and other international organizations have brought wealth but not much style to the capital of Europe. The EU institutions, which occupy about 13 percent of the office space in Brussels, have grown haphazardly in response to the changing nature and the successive enlargements of the 15-nation community. It was only in 1992 that heads of government decided once and for all that Brussels would be the EU's permanent home and it is only since comparatively recently that serious thought has been given to the impact this decision will have on the city, at least from an architectural point of view.

1998-10-24.title: As Deal Came Together, a Snag Over Release of an American Spy
1998-10-24.abstract: Last minute demand by Israel that US free convicted Israeli spy Jonathan Pollard almost resulted in collapse of peace agreement reached between Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat and Israel's Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu; Netanyahu reportedly threatened to refuse to sign agreement withoutPollard's release, even as Israeli television and radio stations reported his freedom had already been won, infuriating Americans; Clinton Administration officials acknowledge fate of former Navy intelligence analyst had been discussed, but insist issue was never viewed as condition of peace agreement; Pollard is serving life sentence for spying for Isrealin 1980's (M)

1998-10-24.title: ARAFAT AND NETANYAHU IN PACT ON NEXT STEPS TOWARD PEACE; MODEST DEAL TO 'REBUILD TRUST'
1998-10-24.abstract: Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat and Israel's Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu, after nine days of tortuous negotiations, sign modest agreement restoring life to Middle East peace effort and opening way for talks on final resolution of 50-year-old fight over possible Palestinian state; Pres Clinton presides over signing ceremony at White House, joined as host by visibly ailing King Hussein of Jordan; agreement in part serves to commit both sides to actions already promised in 1993 and 1995 agreements reached in Oslo; sets detailed timetable for withdrawal of Israeli forces from 13 percent of West Bank in three phases over 12 weeks, beginning soon after ratification, bringing to 40 percent total territory Palestinians control fully or partly; provides for specific steps by Palestinians to fight terrorism and formally amend charter to expunge clauses calling for Israel's destruction; Arafat pledges never to leave peace process or to return to violence and confrontation; Netanyahu says agreement will make Israel and entire region secure; Clinton urges both to carry out commitments and to have courage enough to withstand inevitable attacks from enemies of peace; photos (M)

1998-10-24.title: World Briefing
1998-10-24.abstract: EUROPE    ITALY: VOTE OF CONFIDENCE -- Prime Minister Massimo D'Alema won a vote of confidence from the Chamber of Deputies, the lower house of Parliament, for his newly formed government, the first to include Communists in Italy since 1947.   Alessandra Stanley (NYT)    RUSSIA: AN OPTIMISTIC YELTSIN -- President Boris N. Yeltsin said that Russia's economic plight remained difficult but that the country was already starting down the long road out of crisis. Mr. Yeltsin's views are not shared by most officials. Russia is deep in recession, has no clear recovery plan and is threatening to print more money if it does not secure foreign loans. An aide also said that Mr. Yeltsin is fed up with speculation over his ailments and feels hurt that even former allies have fanned the gossip about his health.   (Reuters, Agence France-Presse)

1998-10-24.title: New Role As Umpire May Bring More Risk
1998-10-24.abstract: Middle East peace agreement gives Central Intelligence Agency a new and potentially risky role in safeguarding security for Israel and Palestinian Authority; pact empowers intelligence operatives to referee disputes involving handling of suspected terrorists, to monitor who is arrested and jailed by Israeli and Palestinian authorities and to help establish sophisticated and reliable border checkpoints (M)

1998-10-24.title: After All the Talk, a Hesitant Peace
1998-10-24.abstract: News analysis of Wye Memorandum, new peace agreement reached between Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat and Israel's Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu; both leaders may have crossed painful and fateful political threshold, despite technical and tentative nature of actual arrangements and despite certainty that they will be violated (M)

1998-10-25.title: European Leaders at Summit Greet Arafat and Hail Mideast Pact
1998-10-25.abstract: European Union leaders attending summit meeting in Alps welcome Yasir Arafat and praise agreement on Israeli withdrawal from additional Palestinian territory as triumph for Pres Clinton; Arafat briefs European leaders on accord and thanks them for years of political and economic aid to Palestinian cause; he and European leaders praise Clinton for his crucial role in reaching new accord (M)

1998-10-25.title: Koreas Take A First Step To Work Out Formal Peace
1998-10-25.abstract: United States, China and two Koreas agree on first step toward long-range goal of permanent peace on divided Korean peninsula; working parties will be set up to explore peace treaty to replace armistice that has existed since end of Korean War in 1953, and to take steps to reduce tensions on most heavily defended border in world; progress is made in separate, bilateral discussions between United States and North Korean officials, regarding American concerns that large North Korean underground installation could be used for nuclear purposes; official United States visit to site could be announced soon (M)

1998-10-25.title: Deaths in Argentina Feed a Taste for Conspiracy
1998-10-25.abstract: Argentines are treated to barrage of recent news coverage about suspicious deaths; deaths may simply be series of macabre concidences, but news reports have spawned vague suggestions lately that organized crime or Government of Pres Carlos Saul Menem itself is involved and blocking cause of justice; recent deaths of former presidential aide's brother and navy captain were officially attributed to suicide; list of other dead include general and another army officer who were killed in helicopter crash, two other Government and military employees who died in reported criminal assaults, and several more people who reportedly succumbed to heart attacks and brain seizures; even Pres Menem has called for reopening of probe into death of his son, thought to have been killed in helicopter accident three years ago; Menem's former wife has long expressed skepticism that crash was accident; photo (M)

1998-10-25.title: U.S. PLANS TO SEND BILLIONS TO SHIELD BRAZIL'S ECONOMY
1998-10-25.abstract: United States will reportedly contribute several billion dollars in direct aid or loan guarantees as part of $30 billion global effort to insulate Brazil, and with it rest of Latin America, from worst effects of financial turmoil circling globe; details of American contribution have yet to be negotiated, but Congressional leaders have been alerted to likelihood that Clinton Administration will act while Congress is in recess; biggest role in rescue program for Brazil will be taken by International Monetary Fund, which will contribute at least $15 billion; another $9 billion will come from World Bank and Inter-American Development Bank (M)

1998-10-25.title: Mideast Marathon: How 9 Days of Talks Ended in 'the Long Night'
1998-10-25.abstract: Participants at Wye River Middle East conference recall most dramatic moments of all-night stand during which agreement was finally clinched between Israel and Palestinians; say turning point was when Pres Clinton became so angry at Israeli Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu that he left room; Netanyahu relented, Clinton returned, and negotiations went into crucial home stretch; other incidents of high drama and some moments of levity recalled (M)

1998-10-26.title: Europe's Leaders End Meeting With Call for Interest Rate Cuts to Fight Slump
1998-10-26.abstract: European Union leaders end summit conference, Klagenfurt, Austria, with broad accord on easing economic and monetary policies to guard against world recession; Germany, under new leadership of Chancellor-elect Gerhard Schroder, and other nations that have followed German lead in cutting Government deficits to get ready for common currency, now seem set to press independent European central bank to let them use euro to spur economic growth (M)

1998-10-26.title: Japanese Are Torn Between Efficiency and Egalitarian Values
1998-10-26.abstract: Japanese drive to become more efficient to revive economy also poses threat to egalitarianism and other key values; Japan's commitment to equality is producing welfare state for inefficient, leading to growing misallocation of resources; spending to improve quality of life in remote areas helped boost budget deficit, spurring tax increase that plunged Japan into recession; heavy spending on remote school for one pupil is an example of glaring inefficiencies; graph of after-tax income distribution in major economies; maps; photos (M)

1998-10-26.title: Turkey Places New Limits on Oil Tanker Traffic in Bosporus
1998-10-26.abstract: Turkey sets new cubs on use of Bosporus, increasing pressure on oil companies to route pipeline to carry Caspian Sea oil through Turkey to port of Ceyhan; Clinton Administration favors Turkish route, which oil companies say is too expensive for now; map (M)

1998-10-26.title: Election of Rivals in Brazil Hurts Cardoso Reform Plans
1998-10-26.abstract: Rivals of Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso of Brazil win key gubernatorial races, complicating his effort to push through long-delayed government and economic reforms; Gov Mario Covas of Sao Paulo, endorsed by Cardoso, seems likely to win re-election over Paulo Maluf, whose victory could have further hurt Cardoso's reform effort (M)

1998-10-26.title: C.I.A.'s Role in Mideast Peace Prompts Outcry and a Call for Senate Hearings
1998-10-26.abstract: Sen Richard C Shelby plans to have his Select Committee on Intelligence hold hearings on Central Intelligence Agency's role in new Israeli-Palestinian peace accord; expresses concern about CIA's open new role in verifying accord as departure from traditional role of intelligence gathering; State Sec Madeleine K Albright says CIA is already engaged in similar work, especially in Middle East; says CIA Dir George J Tenet will explain issues to Congress (M)

1998-10-27.title: World Briefing
1998-10-27.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: CAN'T PAY DEBT -- Russia's Government will owe $17.5 billion in foreign debt next year but won't have sufficient hard currency to pay it, unless Russia first receives international aid, said Aleksandr Zhukov, head of Parliament's budget committee. Mr. Zhukov said tax receipts and other revenues for the first nine months of the year amounted to only 51 percent of targeted totals.    (AP)    RUSSIA: ELECTRICITY SHUT OFF -- At least 76 freight trains were stuck for hours on an eastern stretch of the Trans-Siberian railway after the local electricity company cut off the power for non-payment of bills. Power was restored late in the day, although there was no indication that the $3 million owed had been paid. (Agence France-Presse)    GEORGIA: ARMY BACKING -- President Eduard She-  vardnadze of Georgia, left, defended a decision to use the army to crush a military revolt last week without waiting for parliamentary approval. Mr. Shevardnadze said the revolt took place in a military unit, so the army had been responsible for re-establishing order. (Reuters)    CZECH REPUBLIC: HONOR WITHDRAWN -- President Vaclav Havel has withdrawn a high Czech honor, the Order of the White Lion, which was to be given to the former Mayor of Vienna, Helmut Zilk, because of charges that he was on the payroll of the Communist secret police in the 1960's. Mr. Zilk denied the allegations. (Reuters)    ASIA    AFGHANISTAN: LOOKING AT BIN LADEN -- The Taliban said it is looking into the activities of Osama bin Laden, the wealthy Saudi accused of involvement in the bombings of the American Embassies in Kenya and Tanzania, the BBC reported. It quoted a Taliban spokesman as saying that Mr. bin Laden, who is believed to live in Afghanistan, was not involved in the attacks, but the allegations needed to be cleared up.    INDIA: ONION EXPORTS BANNED -- The Indian Government banned the export of onions, the country's favorite vegetable, after a lingering shortage caused a more than 500 percent jump in its  price. The move comes ahead of local elections next month in New Delhi and three states, where the spiraling price of onions has become a major headache for the Hindu nationalist-led ruling coalition. (Agence France-Presse)    INDIA: 'STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE' -- Prime Minister Atal Behari Vajpayee said India's nuclear tests last May were a decisive step toward taking India's ''rightful place'' in a multi-polar world. A Government statement quoted Mr. Vajpayee as telling a conference of military commanders that the series of underground explosions had ''far-reaching strategic significance.'' (Reuters)    JAPAN: BLEAK PROSPECTS -- Japan's prospects for economic recovery remain bleak, and bad debts at its financial institutions threaten further harm to employment and income, the chief of the country's central bank said. Japan's banks are saddled with an estimated $1 trillion in bad debts left over from excessive lending during the 1980's. (AP)    SINGAPORE: ZERO GROWTH -- Singapore's economy will probably have zero growth in 1999, after shrinking in the last two quarters of this year, Deputy Prime Minister Lee Hsien Loong said. Singapore's economy is shrinking dramatically, with growth at 1.6 percent in the second quarter of this year, down from 6.1 percent in the first quarter. (AP)    MYANMAR: FACING MORE SANCTIONS -- The European Union decided to step up sanctions against the ruling junta in Myanmar. The group's foreign ministers, meeting in Luxembourg, also said they backed the view of the country's opposition leader Aung San Suu Kyi that ''in the present situation, it is inappropriate for tourists to visit Burma,'' as Myanmar was previously called. (Agence France-Presse)    AFRICA    CONGO: NO REBELS AT TALKS -- African defense and foreign ministers began talks in Lusaka aimed at securing a cease-fire in Congo, but rebels fighting to topple President Laurent Kabila were shut out. President Frederick Chiluba of Zambia told ministers from 19 African nations that the only item on the agenda was ending the war in the central African country before conflict spread beyond its borders. (Reuters)    NIGERIA: SUPPORTING CIVILIAN RULE -- The military ruler, Gen. Abdulsalami Abubakar, has warned soldiers not to intervene in Nigeria's planned transition to democracy. ''The Nigerian Army must remain a stabilizing factor ready to do our best to insure continuity,'' General Abubakar said in a leaflet handed out to troops. ''Military action is subordinate to political will.'' (AP)    THE AMERICAS    UNITED NATIONS: SANCTIONS ATTACKED -- The General Assembly adopted a Libyan-sponsored resolution, aimed at the United States, calling for the immediate repeal of laws that unilaterally impose sanctions on companies and nationals of other countries. The vote was 80 in favor, with only the United States and Israel voting against. But 67 delegations abstained, an unusually large number. (Reuters)    PANAMA: HEIR TO RULING PARTY -- Eager to retain power through the American handover of the Panama Canal on Dec. 31, 1999, Panama's ruling Democratic Revolutionary Party has nominated the 35-year-old son of Gen. Omar Torrijos, the country's former strongman, as its candidate in the presidential election next May 2. In primary balloting on Sunday, Martin Torrijos Espino handily defeated nine other aspirants. Larry Rohter (NYT)    ARGENTINA: BRITISH OLIVE BRANCH -- The British  Government offered an olive branch on the eve of a visit by Argentina's President, Carlos Menem, the first since the Falklands war, with Foreign Secretary Robin Cook hinting in London that an arms embargo may be relaxed soon. The embargo has been in place since 1982, when Argentine forces occupied the Falkland Islands, which Argentines call Las Malvinas, and were then expelled by British forces in a war that killed more than 900 men. (Agence France-Presse)    CUBA: KEEPING U.S. REPORTERS OUT -- President Fidel Castro said he was not ready to let American journalists operate permanently in Cuba. ''Once they are established it is hard to remove them,'' Mr. Castro told the leadership of the American Society of Newspaper Editors during a six-hour meeting in Havana. (AP)    BRAZIL: MIXED RESULT FOR CARDOSO -- President Fernando Henrique Cardoso's plan to pull Brazil out of economic turmoil received a strong boost with the re-election of a key ally, Gov. Mario Covas, as Governor of Sao Paulo, Brazil's largest state. But opposition candidates took 5 of the other 12 governorships in voting on Sunday, which many Brazilians saw as a referendum on Cardoso's austerity measures. (AP)    CANADA: EX-LEADER FALLS SHORT -- Former Prime Minister Joe Clark has been forced into a second round in his bid to regain the leadership of the Conservative party. Over the weekend, Mr. Clark, who was Prime Minister of Canada for nine months in 1979, far outdistanced four rivals with 48.7 percent of the party's vote but fell just short of the majority needed to win. A second ballot is expected on Nov. 14. Anthony DePalma (NYT)    CANADA: LOGGERS END BLOCKADE -- A group of Maine loggers angry about a Federal program that allows Canadian woodsmen to work in their state ended a weeklong blockade along the Quebec border today after being threatened with arrest. In agreeing to leave, the loggers said they would give the fEderal and state Governments two weeks to act on their complaints. (AP)    MEXICO: A VICTORY FOR PRI -- Tomas Yarrington Ruvalcaba, the candidate from President Ernesto Zedillo's party, known as the PRI, has won election by a wide margin as Governor of the northeastern state of Tamaulipas, which borders Texas, according to preliminary results of balloting on Sunday.
